storm.summary_layperson: Isolated thunderstorms are possible late this afternoon and evening as a heat wave peaks, but the cool Sound will likely weaken any storm that tries to cross the coast — expect heat and haze with only a small chance a cell reaches the cove.
storm.local_effect_blurb: Cool Sound water (72°F) against 88°F inland air is a 16°F gap, strong enough to form a stable marine layer that often chokes incoming thunderstorms at the shoreline.
storm.detail_html:
Regional picture: NWS has an Extreme Heat Warning in effect through Thursday evening; heat indices climb to ~109°F this afternoon. Storm potential is a background risk, not the headline — the AFD calls today's severe risk Marginal to Slight with damaging wind as the primary threat (DCAPE up to 2000 J/kg). CAPE is 2320 J/kg with 34 kt of 0–6 km shear from the NW. Radar is currently quiet within 50 mi.

Local modifier active: water_air_temp_delta is +15.7°F, marine layer strength strong, sound_effect_likelihood moderate, protective_mechanism_active = true. A stable layer sits over Long Island Sound, and any cell that tries to cross the coast tends to lose its low-level fuel.

Most likely outcome: if a storm fires this evening, expect the cove to feel outflow wind but with the rain/thunder core weaker or displaced — surrounding inland areas (Stamford, White Plains) may get hit harder. The forecast is only 20% precip; a completely dry evening is more likely than a hit.

What would change the call: if a cell tracks in from the northwest over land (bypassing the Sound), the marine layer buys nothing — treat any NW-approach warning at face value. Same if CAPE spikes above 3000 J/kg (RAP fallback already reads 2970, close to the punch-through gate) — vigorous updrafts can override the modifier.

hourly.shift_note: sea breeze ramping, right shift continuing

wind.tactics_blurb: SSW thermal + synoptic tailwind — clean flow up the cove axis with a mature right-shift signature through 19:00. Get the first two races in; the thermal collapses fast after sunset (~20:30) and 21:00 finish lines will be drifting.
wind.tactics_detail_html:
Regime call: A+ (thermal with synoptic tailwind). Inland–water deltaT is 15.7°F (huge), coastal_pct is 0.53 (developing/borderline moderate — SB still ramping through afternoon), and the synoptic gradient at 8.9 kt SSW is adding to the thermal rather than fighting it. Classic western-LIS afternoon: SB and gradient stacked in the same direction.

Favored side — right. With SSW–SW flow, the cove opens due south so the flow is clean straight up the axis. During the ramping phase of a SB (before ~15:00 local, and again as it matures 17:00–19:00) the direction shifts steadily clockwise, so right-side leverage on the beat pays. West shore has slight channeling but stronger pressure is toward the middle-right of the course as you head up.

Shift character: in a mature SB you'd play oscillations, but with deltaT this large the thermal is still building through the early races — expect a persistent right shift through 19:00 (ramping SB behavior), then oscillations around a mean ~215° for one race, then collapse. Do not commit hard left early. HRRR/obs divergence is only 13° (below the trust-obs threshold) — direction confidence high, forecast direction ~212° SSW.

Sunset collapse: sunset today ~20:30 EDT. HRRR drops the SSW flow from 9 kt at 19:00 to 3 kt at 21:00 — that's a fast thermal death. Expect wind to drop below 5 kt within 20 minutes of sunset. Any race starting after 20:00 risks finishing in a hole.

What the fleet feels: 6–10 kt SSW through the first two races — powered-up 420/Laser sailing, dinghies in the sweet spot. Bigger boats will want the SSW to hold. Third race is a gamble.

data_caveats: Buoy 44040 wind unavailable (last reading 72 min ago) — buoy subregion in the SB depth check has only 1 sample.
Sea breeze fractions borderline (coastal_pct 0.53) — call is a developing SB backed by 15.7°F deltaT.
HRRR: 1 forecast hour flagged fetch/parse warning (pipeline still usable).
